turns.content: I've been reading about Rust bindings for Node.js but I still don't get how to actually connect them. Is there a simple way to call a Rust function from JavaScript?

The main crate you need is napi-rs, and its core building block is the napi macro which lets you define functions that bridge into Node.js. You would use napi::bind_runtime() to attach your Rust code to the runtime, then write a function using #[napi] to expose it.
Wait, so I can just put my Rust function in main.rs and it will automatically run when Node.js starts? That sounds too easy.
Not quite. You still need to compile your library with napi-build which handles the build script, generates the shared object file, and registers the symbols that Node.js can load at runtime.
Oh right, I thought the macro did everything including building. But what about async functions? Can Rust really do async?
Yes, napi-rs supports async functions using #[napi] with the async trait or by wrapping futures in a special way so they can be called from JavaScript.
But how does JS call it? Do I need to write some glue code?
You don't write glue code. The napi macro generates the necessary bindings automatically, and you just export your functions with #[napi] in your Rust crate.
What about types? Can I pass complex objects like arrays or maps from JS to Rust?
Absolutely. napi-rs has built-in support for most JavaScript types, so you can receive an array of numbers or a map of strings in your #[napi] function arguments.
But what if I need to create something new? Like a custom data structure?
You can define your own Rust structs and implement the necessary traits, then pass them through napi-rs. The macro will handle serialization automatically.
